title: Two Sum II (sorted)
code: public int[] TwoSumSorted(int[] numbers, int target)
right--;   // sum too big -> need a smaller value
left++;    // sum too small -> need a bigger value

frames.caption: Initialize the converging interval: left = 0 (front), right = 3 (back). Walk the two ends toward each other.
frames.note: Converging two pointers needs sorted input: that sort order is what tells us which pointer to move. left starts at the smallest value, right at the largest, and the interval [left, right] holds every pair still worth testing. The loop guard is left < right (strict) so a number never pairs with itself and the pointers stop the instant they meet.

frames.caption: sum = numbers[0] + numbers[3] = 2 + 15 = 17. Compare against target 9.
frames.note: Compute sum = numbers[L] + numbers[R], the pair formed by the two ends still in play. Because the array is sorted ascending, numbers[R] is the largest right partner available and numbers[L] the smallest left partner — so this one comparison tells us, in O(1), which whole half of the remaining pairs to discard. That is the converging-pointer win over the O(n^2) nested scan.

frames.caption: sum = 17 > 9: the right value is too big for any remaining left. Retire it — right-- = 2.
frames.note: sum > target with numbers[R] the largest remaining value: even paired with the smallest left it already overshoots, so numbers[R] overshoots with every remaining left too — column R can never hit the target. Retire it with R-- (the only move that can lower the sum), which strictly shrinks the interval and guarantees termination.

frames.caption: sum = numbers[0] + numbers[2] = 2 + 11 = 13. Compare against target 9.
frames.note: Compute sum = numbers[L] + numbers[R], the pair formed by the two ends still in play. Because the array is sorted ascending, numbers[R] is the largest right partner available and numbers[L] the smallest left partner — so this one comparison tells us, in O(1), which whole half of the remaining pairs to discard. That is the converging-pointer win over the O(n^2) nested scan.

frames.caption: sum = 13 > 9: the right value is too big for any remaining left. Retire it — right-- = 1.
frames.note: sum > target with numbers[R] the largest remaining value: even paired with the smallest left it already overshoots, so numbers[R] overshoots with every remaining left too — column R can never hit the target. Retire it with R-- (the only move that can lower the sum), which strictly shrinks the interval and guarantees termination.

frames.caption: sum = numbers[0] + numbers[1] = 2 + 7 = 9. Compare against target 9.
frames.note: Compute sum = numbers[L] + numbers[R], the pair formed by the two ends still in play. Because the array is sorted ascending, numbers[R] is the largest right partner available and numbers[L] the smallest left partner — so this one comparison tells us, in O(1), which whole half of the remaining pairs to discard. That is the converging-pointer win over the O(n^2) nested scan.

frames.caption: sum = 9 == target. Found the pair — return 1-indexed positions [1, 2].
frames.note: Equality is the win condition. LC 167 asks for 1-indexed positions, so we return left + 1 and right + 1, not the raw indices. This converging solution is O(n) time and O(1) space — the same time as the hash-map Two Sum (LC 1) but without its O(n) extra map, because sortedness lets us decide each move from a single comparison.
